title: The Remaining Bottleneck
concept: Fast R-CNN is fast but still bottlenecked by the classical CPU Selective Search algorithm used to generate candidate regions.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the benefit of smooth L1 loss in bounding box regression?

options.text: It is less sensitive to outlier coordinate errors, preventing gradient explosion

exercises.explain: Smooth L1 loss is robust to large errors, stabilizing box coordinate regression.

cards.title: Waiting at the Gate
cards.body: If you buy a ticket for a hyper-fast roller coaster, but the gatekeeper at the entrance has to check your ID by hand-writing your name in a paper ledger, the high speed of the ride doesn't matter. You are still stuck in a slow queue. This is the issue Fast R-CNN faced.

cards.title: The Proposal Bottleneck
cards.body: Although Fast R-CNN accelerated CNN feature extraction, the region proposals were still generated by Selective Search on the CPU. This step takes about 2.0 seconds, dwarfing the 0.2 seconds the GPU takes for the rest of the network.

cards.title: A Slow Clerk and a Fast Chef
cards.body: Imagine a restaurant with a kitchen that can cook a meal in 10 seconds (GPU). But the waiter takes 2 minutes to write down the order on paper (CPU). The total meal time is still limited by the waiter, not the chef!

cards.title: Latency Breakdown
cards.body: In Fast R-CNN, the forward pass of the network takes 0.2s. But generating the candidate proposals using Selective Search on the CPU takes 2.0s. Thus, 90% of the processing time is wasted on proposal generation.

cards.title: Not fully end-to-end
cards.body: It's a myth that Fast R-CNN is a fully end-to-end deep learning system. Because it relies on an external, classical CPU pixel-grouping algorithm (Selective Search) to generate proposals, it is a hybrid system.

cards.title: Fast R-CNN Time Breakdown
cards.body: Visualizing the time distribution in the Fast R-CNN pipeline:

left.text:
Takes 2.0 seconds (90% of time).
No GPU acceleration.

exercises.prompt: What was the remaining speed bottleneck in Fast R-CNN?
exercises.explain: Even though Fast R-CNN optimized the classifier, it still depended on Selective Search on CPU, which took ~2 seconds.

options.text: Selective Search running on the CPU to generate region proposals
RoI Pooling taking too much GPU memory
The classification SVMs being too deep
The backbone CNN being too slow on the GPU

exercises.prompt: True or False: In Fast R-CNN, proposal generation takes more time than CNN classification during inference.
exercises.explain: True. Selective Search takes about 2.0s, while the CNN and detection heads take only about 0.2s.
Selective Search is an external, classical algorithm outside of the neural network graph.
exercises.sentence: Fast R-CNN is not fully end-to-end because proposal generation is handled by an ___ algorithm.

exercises.prompt: Which step in the Fast R-CNN pipeline prevents it from running at real-time speeds (30 fps)?
exercises.explain: The 2-second CPU delay from Selective Search blocks the network from reaching real-time speeds.

options.text: Selective Search on CPU taking 2 seconds

exercises.prompt: You want to achieve real-time object detection at 30 frames per second. How must you redesign the region proposal step to bypass the CPU bottleneck?

options.text: Replace Selective Search with a lightweight neural network that runs on the GPU and predicts proposals directly from the shared feature map.
Run Selective Search on a multi-core CPU cluster.
Only run Selective Search on every 100th frame.
Use data augmentation to shrink the images to 10x10 pixels.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Introducing a neural network that predicts proposals on the GPU (sharing features with the backbone) is the key. Next: let's study the architecture that did this: Faster R-CNN!
